questions.question: What is the definition of menopause from a clinical point of view?
options: Cessation of menstruation for one year
questions.explanation: Menopause is clinically defined as the permanent cessation of menstruation for 12 consecutive months, without another pathological cause. It is a retrospective diagnosis.

questions.question: What period includes the female climacteric?
questions.explanation: The climacteric encompasses the menopausal transition (perimenopause) and the years following menopause, including progressive hormonal and clinical changes.

questions.question: What is the average age of natural menopause?
questions.explanation: The average age of natural menopause is 51 years, although it can vary between 45 and 55 years. It is considered premature before the age of 40.

questions.question: What characterizes perimenopause?
questions.explanation: Perimenopause is characterized by variable menstrual cycles, frequently anovulatory, with hormonal fluctuations that generate symptoms such as hot flashes and menstrual irregularity.

questions.question: What is menopause that occurs before the age of 40 called?
questions.explanation: Early menopause or premature ovarian failure is defined as the cessation of menstruation before the age of 40, which can be spontaneous or induced.

questions.question: What is the main hormonal cause of vasomotor symptoms in the climacteric?
questions.explanation: The decrease in estrogen levels during the climacteric alters the hypothalamic thermoregulatory center, triggering hot flashes and night sweats.

questions.question: What is the average age of natural menopause in women in Western countries?
questions.explanation: Natural menopause occurs on average at age 51 in Western countries, defined by 12 months of consecutive amenorrhea without pathological cause.

questions.question: What age range is considered early menopause?
questions.explanation: Early menopause or premature ovarian failure is defined when it occurs before the age of 40, differentiating it from early menopause (40-45 years).

questions.question: What is the main factor that determines the age of natural menopause?
questions.explanation: The age of natural menopause is determined mainly by the genetic ovarian reserve, although factors such as smoking can advance it slightly.

questions.question: What hormone is characteristically elevated in perimenopause as a marker of follicular exhaustion?
questions.explanation: In perimenopause, FSH rises in a compensatory manner due to the decrease in inhibin and follicular reserve, being an early marker of ovarian exhaustion.

questions.question: Which of the following factors is associated with later menopause?
questions.explanation: Obesity is associated with later menopause due to greater peripheral conversion of androgens to estrogens in adipose tissue, prolonging ovarian function.

questions.question: What approximate percentage of women experience natural menopause between the ages of 40 and 44?
questions.explanation: About 5% of women experience early menopause (40-44 years), while early menopause (<40 years) occurs in approximately 1%.

questions.question: What is the definition of natural menopause?
questions.explanation: Natural menopause is defined retrospectively after 12 consecutive months of amenorrhea, without another pathological cause, reflecting ovarian follicular depletion.

questions.question: What characterizes perimenopause?
questions.explanation: Perimenopause includes the transition period with irregular menstrual cycles, vasomotor symptoms and hormonal changes, beginning years before menopause.

questions.question: When does postmenopause start?
questions.explanation: Postmenopause begins immediately after menopause, that is, after 12 months of amenorrhea, and covers the rest of a woman's life.

questions.question: What is the typical hormonal marker of postmenopause?
questions.explanation: In postmenopause, the loss of ovarian feedback increases serum FSH (>25 IU/L) and decreases estradiol (<20 pg/mL), confirming the hypoestrogenic state.

questions.question: What symptom is most characteristic of perimenopause?
questions.explanation: Hot flashes and night sweats are the most common vasomotor symptoms during perimenopause, due to the fluctuation and decrease in estrogen.

questions.question: What is the average age of natural menopause?
questions.explanation: Natural menopause occurs on average at age 51, with a normal range between 45 and 55 years; Before 40 it is considered premature menopause.

questions.question: What is the main diagnostic criterion for natural menopause?
questions.explanation: Natural menopause is diagnosed after 12 consecutive months of amenorrhea, without any other pathological cause. This clinical criterion is the internationally accepted standard.

questions.question: What hormone is typically elevated in confirmed menopause?
questions.explanation: In menopause, the cessation of ovarian function reduces estradiol, which eliminates negative feedback on the pituitary, raising FSH (>25-40 mIU/mL).

questions.question: What is the average age of natural menopause?
questions.explanation: The average age of natural menopause is 51 years, with a normal range between 45 and 55 years. Values ​​outside this range may indicate early or late menopause.

questions.question: What history invalidates the 12-month amenorrhea criterion?
questions.explanation: Hysterectomy eliminates menstruation, so 12-month amenorrhea cannot be used as a criterion; Elevated serum FSH and vasomotor symptoms are required.

questions.question: What symptom usually accompanies 12-month amenorrhea?
questions.explanation: Hot flashes and night sweats are typical vasomotor symptoms of the climacteric, secondary to the decrease in estrogen, and frequently accompany 12-month amenorrhea.

questions.question: What study is recommended before confirming menopause in 12-month amenorrhea?
questions.explanation: The thyroid and prolactin profile rule out secondary causes of amenorrhea (such as hypothyroidism or hyperprolactinemia) before attributing amenorrhea to natural menopause.

questions.question: What is the main pathophysiology of hot flashes in menopause?
questions.explanation: Hot flashes are due to a decrease in estrogen, which alters the hypothalamic thermoregulatory center and causes peripheral vasodilation and sweating.

questions.question: What is the first-line pharmacological treatment for moderate to severe hot flashes?
questions.explanation: Hormone therapy with estrogens (alone or in combination) is the most effective treatment for moderate to severe vasomotor symptoms in menopause.

questions.question: What is the typical duration of untreated hot flashes during menopause?
questions.explanation: Untreated hot flashes usually last on average 4 to 7 years, although the duration varies depending on individual and ethnic factors.

questions.question: Which of the following is a risk factor for more severe hot flashes?
questions.explanation: Surgical menopause (bilateral oophorectomy) causes a sharp drop in estrogen, which is associated with more intense and frequent hot flashes.

questions.question: What non-hormonal alternative is used for hot flashes in patients with an estrogenic contraindication?
questions.explanation: Clonidine, an alpha-2 adrenergic agonist, is a non-hormonal option for hot flashes, although with moderate efficacy and adverse effects such as dry mouth.

questions.question: What clinical finding characterizes a typical hot flash?
questions.explanation: Typical hot flashes present as a sudden feeling of heat, followed by facial flushing and sweating, which can occur both day and night.

questions.question: What is the main cause of urogenital atrophy in menopause?
questions.explanation: Urogenital atrophy in menopause is mainly due to the decrease in estrogen, which causes thinning of the vaginal and urethral epithelium, decreased vascularization and lubrication, generating symptoms such as dryness and dyspareunia.

questions.question: What symptom is characteristic of urogenital atrophy?
questions.explanation: Superficial dyspareunia is a cardinal symptom of urogenital atrophy, due to the dryness and fragility of the vaginal epithelium due to estrogen deficiency, which causes pain during penetration.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment for urogenital atrophy?
questions.explanation: Topical estrogens are the first-line treatment for urogenital atrophy, as they restore the vaginal epithelium, improve lubrication, and relieve dyspareunia, with minimal systemic absorption and low risk.

questions.question: Which finding on pelvic examination suggests urogenital atrophy?
questions.explanation: Pale, thin vaginal epithelium with loss of folds is a classic finding of urogenital atrophy, a consequence of estrogen deficiency, unlike inflammatory or neoplastic findings.

questions.question: What urinary complication is associated with urogenital atrophy?
questions.explanation: Stress urinary incontinence is associated with urogenital atrophy due to weakness of urethral support and decreased sphincter tone, due to lack of estrogen, affecting quality of life.

questions.question: What non-hormonal risk factor aggravates urogenital atrophy?
questions.explanation: Active tobacco aggravates urogenital atrophy due to its antiestrogenic and vasoconstrictive effect, reducing vaginal vascularization and worsening the symptoms of dryness and dyspareunia.

questions.question: What hormone is the main protector of bone mass in premenopausal women?
questions.explanation: Estrogen inhibits osteoclastic activity and promotes bone formation; Its deficiency during menopause accelerates the loss of bone mass and the risk of osteoporosis.

questions.question: What is the key pathophysiological mechanism of estrogen deficiency in osteoporosis?
questions.explanation: The lack of estrogen eliminates the brake on osteoclasts, increasing bone resorption and generating a negative balance that thins the trabecular bone.

questions.question: Which laboratory test is typically altered in postmenopausal osteoporosis?
questions.explanation: In postmenopausal osteoporosis, alkaline phosphatase may be elevated as a marker of increased bone turnover, while calcium, phosphorus, and PTH are usually normal.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment to prevent fractures in osteoporosis due to estrogen deficiency?
questions.explanation: Bisphosphonates (alendronate, risedronate) are the recommended initial therapy due to their antifracture effectiveness; Hormone therapy is reserved for women with moderate to severe menopausal symptoms.

questions.question: What additional risk factor increases osteoporosis in menopausal women?
questions.explanation: Smoking accelerates bone loss by reducing calcium absorption and lowering estrogen levels, increasing the risk of osteoporosis in menopause.

questions.question: What is the most common location of osteoporotic fracture in postmenopausal women?
questions.explanation: Hip fracture (proximal femur) is the most serious and frequent fracture in postmenopausal osteoporosis, followed by vertebrae and wrist, due to the loss of trabecular and cortical bone.

questions.question: What is the main indication for hormone therapy in menopause?
questions.explanation: Hormone therapy is primarily indicated for the relief of moderate to severe vasomotor symptoms associated with menopause, such as hot flashes and night sweats.

questions.question: What is an absolute contraindication for systemic hormone therapy?
questions.explanation: A history of breast cancer is an absolute contraindication to systemic hormone therapy due to the risk of recurrence and tumor stimulation by estrogen.

questions.question: What condition contraindicates the use of estrogen alone in menopause?
questions.explanation: Active endometriosis contraindicates the use of estrogens alone because they can stimulate ectopic endometrial tissue and worsen symptoms.

questions.question: What is the minimum effective dose recommended when starting hormone therapy?
questions.explanation: It is recommended to start with the lowest possible dose that relieves symptoms and minimizes risks, adjusting according to the patient's response and tolerance.

questions.question: What type of hormone therapy is preferred in women with an intact uterus?
questions.explanation: In women with an intact uterus, combined estrogen-progestin therapy is used to prevent endometrial hyperplasia induced by estrogen alone.

questions.question: What is the maximum recommended duration of hormone therapy for vasomotor symptoms?
questions.explanation: It is recommended to use hormone therapy for less than 5 years for vasomotor symptoms, as the risk of adverse events increases with prolonged use.

questions.question: What is the most common cause of abnormal uterine bleeding in perimenopause?
questions.explanation: During perimenopause, persistent anovulation due to alteration of the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis is the most common cause, generating bleeding due to non-secretory proliferative endometrium.

questions.question: What type of uterine fibroid is most frequently associated with abnormal uterine bleeding?
questions.explanation: Submucous myoma, by deforming the endometrial cavity and increasing the bleeding surface, is the one that most frequently causes abnormal uterine bleeding, especially in perimenopause.

questions.question: What is the first-line study to evaluate abnormal uterine bleeding in perimenopause?
questions.explanation: Transvaginal ultrasound is the initial imaging study due to its accessibility and ability to measure endometrial thickness, guiding the need for endometrial biopsy.

questions.question: What endometrial thickness on transvaginal ultrasound justifies biopsy in a perimenopausal woman with bleeding?
questions.explanation: In perimenopausal women with bleeding, the usual cut-off point for indicating an endometrial biopsy is an endometrial thickness ≥ 12 mm, due to the risk of hyperplasia or neoplasia.

questions.question: What is the first-line hormonal treatment for anovulatory bleeding in perimenopause?
questions.explanation: Cyclic progestins (e.g. medroxyprogesterone 10 mg/day for 12-14 days per month) stabilize the anovulatory proliferative endometrium, regulating bleeding and preventing hyperplasia.

questions.question: Which of the following findings on endometrial biopsy indicates the need for hysterectomy?
questions.explanation: Endometrial adenocarcinoma is a malignant neoplasm that requires hysterectomy with surgical staging as standard oncological treatment, unlike hyperplasias that can be managed with progestogens.

questions.question: What is the main mechanism by which SSRIs relieve vasomotor symptoms in menopause?
questions.explanation: SSRIs inhibit the reuptake of serotonin in the synaptic cleft, modulating the hypothalamic thermoregulatory center and reducing hot flashes.

questions.question: Which SSRI is considered first-line for vasomotor symptoms in women with contraindications to hormonal therapy?
questions.explanation: Paroxetine is the SSRI with the greatest evidence for vasomotor symptoms; It is used at low doses when hormone therapy is contraindicated.

questions.question: What is the most common adverse effect associated with the use of SSRIs in menopausal women?
questions.explanation: Nausea and headache are common adverse effects when starting SSRIs; They are usually transitory and decrease with treatment time.

questions.question: What relevant drug interaction should be monitored when prescribing SSRIs in menopausal women?
questions.explanation: SSRIs, especially paroxetine and fluoxetine, inhibit CYP2D6, reducing the activation of tamoxifen and increasing the risk of oncological recurrence.

questions.question: In what typical period is a significant reduction in hot flashes observed with SSRIs in menopause?
questions.explanation: Improvement in vasomotor symptoms with SSRIs is usually evident within 3 to 4 weeks of continuous treatment, although the maximum effect may take longer.

questions.question: What is the recommended starting dose of paroxetine for vasomotor symptoms in menopause?
questions.explanation: The initial dose of paroxetine for vasomotor symptoms is 10 mg/day, adjustable according to response up to a maximum of 20 mg/day.

questions.question: What lipid change characterizes the menopausal transition?
questions.explanation: During menopause, the decrease in estrogen reduces the activity of hepatic LDL receptors, raising its serum levels and increasing the atherogenic risk.

questions.question: What is the main mechanism of increased cardiovascular risk in menopause?
questions.explanation: Estrogen deficiency alters endothelial function, promotes dyslipidemia and favors vascular inflammation, key mechanisms of the increased cardiovascular risk in postmenopause.

questions.question: Which lipoprotein usually increases the most in postmenopause?
questions.explanation: LDL-cholesterol rises significantly after menopause due to the lower expression of hepatic LDL receptors mediated by estrogenic decline, enhancing atherogenesis.

questions.question: What lipid parameter is associated with greater cardiovascular risk in menopausal women?
questions.explanation: High triglycerides in the postmenopause, together with high LDL and low HDL, make up an atherogenic profile that increases cardiovascular risk in this population.

questions.question: What effect does menopausal hormone therapy have on the lipid profile?
questions.explanation: Estrogens administered in hormone therapy improve the lipid profile by reducing LDL-cholesterol and increasing HDL-cholesterol, although the effect on triglycerides depends on the route and type of estrogen.

questions.question: What is the first line of treatment for dyslipidemia in postmenopausal women?
questions.explanation: Statins are the first-line treatment for atherogenic dyslipidemia in postmenopausal women, reducing LDL-cholesterol and cardiovascular events with solid evidence.

questions.question: What FSH value confirms a diagnosis of menopause?
questions.explanation: At menopause, FSH levels exceed 40 mIU/mL due to the loss of negative feedback from estradiol, confirming the cessation of ovarian function.

questions.question: What is the main estrogen that decreases during menopause?
questions.explanation: Estradiol, produced primarily by the ovaries, declines dramatically at menopause, while estrone becomes the dominant estrogen by peripheral aromatization.

questions.question: What laboratory finding is expected in menopause?
questions.explanation: In menopause, ovarian failure reduces estradiol, eliminating negative feedback on the pituitary, which raises FSH significantly.

questions.question: What is the typical estradiol range in menopause?
questions.explanation: In menopause, serum estradiol is usually less than 20 pg/mL, reflecting follicular atrophy and cessation of ovarian estrogen production.

questions.question: What hormone is elevated as a compensatory response during menopause?
questions.explanation: At menopause, LH also rises, although to a lesser extent than FSH, due to the loss of negative feedback from estradiol and inhibin.

questions.question: When is a woman considered to be in menopause according to the laboratory?
questions.explanation: The biochemical diagnosis of menopause is established with FSH persistently greater than 40 mIU/mL and estradiol less than 20 pg/mL, together with amenorrhea of ​​at least 12 months.
